alter.name: Robert Alter
alter.tradition: b. 1935 ·Literary Scholar· University of California, Berkeley · Class of 1937 Professor of Hebrew and Comparative Literature

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Robert Alter was born in New York City in 1935 and studied at Columbia University and Harvard, where he received his doctorate in comparative literature. He has taught at UC Berkeley since 1967, where he holds a chair in Hebrew and Comparative Literature — a unique appointment that reflects the distinctive combination of skills he brought to biblical studies: deep knowledge of modern literature, mastery of classical Hebrew, and sensitivity to the ways narrative art operates across cultures and centuries.

His 1981 book “The Art of Biblical Narrative” transformed the study of the Hebrew Bible in the English-speaking world. It demonstrated, with close textual argument, that the narrative techniques of Genesis, Samuel, and the other prose books were not primitive or merely functional but constituted a sophisticated literary system — comparable in artistry to Homer or Tolstoy, and demanding the same quality of readerly attention.

His later book “The Art of Biblical Poetry” (1985) did the same for the psalms, prophets, and wisdom literature. And his three-volume translation and commentary, “The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary” (2018), is the culmination of a fifty-year project — a fresh translation from the Hebrew that attempts to preserve the stylistic and rhythmic qualities of the original, accompanied by detailed literary and philological notes.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Alter reads the Hebrew Bible as literature — not in the reductive sense of “merely” literature, but in the sense that its literary choices are also its theological choices. The way a narrative is structured, what it repeats and what it omits, how it develops character through dialogue and action rather than description — these are not decorative features but the primary vehicles of meaning.

His most important insight is the concept of “type scenes” — conventional narrative patterns (the betrothal at a well, the annunciation of birth to a barren woman, the deathbed blessing) that the biblical writers use, vary, and play against expectation to create meaning. When Jacob meets Rachel at a well in Genesis 29, the reader familiar with the type scene knows something significant is happening — and the ways the story confirms or surprises those expectations carry theological weight.

He is also the finest English analyst of biblical Hebrew prose style: the deliberate use of repetition, the “contrastive focus” of parallel clauses, the extraordinary economy of description that forces meaning into gesture and dialogue. His translations try to preserve these features in ways the NIV, ESV, and other committee translations inevitably smooth away.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Alter is Jewish but writes from a secular-academic rather than a confessional perspective. He does not approach the text as divine revelation in the way MacArthur, Sarna, or Calvin do. But his literary attentiveness produces insights that deeply inform theological reading — because the literary choices of the biblical authors are often the precise mechanisms by which theological claims are made. To miss the art is to miss the theology it carries.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The Art of Biblical Narrative (1981)The foundational text of literary approaches to the Hebrew Bible. Changed how a generation of scholars and readers read Genesis and Samuel.

The Art of Biblical Poetry (1985)The companion volume for the poetic books — Psalms, Proverbs, Job, the prophets. Particularly powerful on the dynamics of biblical parallelism.

The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary (3 vols., 2018)A lifetime’s work: fresh translation with extensive literary and philological notes. The primary source for Alter commentary in this tool.

The David Story (1999)Translation and commentary on Samuel — Alter’s most sustained reading of a single narrative sequence, widely regarded as a masterpiece of biblical scholarship.

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body:
Alter notes currently appear in Genesis 1. Coverage will expand across Genesis and other narrative books as the retrofit progresses.

anderson.name: A.A. Anderson
anderson.tradition: 1927–2009 ·OT Scholar· University of Manchester · WBC 2 Samuel

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: A.A. (Arnold Albert) Anderson was a British OT scholar at the University of Manchester known for careful, philologically grounded commentary on the Hebrew text.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body: Anderson reads 2 Samuel with close attention to the Hebrew text, text-critical questions, and the literary structure of the Succession Narrative. His WBC commentary provides detailed verse-by-verse analysis with extensive engagement with ancient versions and modern scholarship.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Anderson writes from a centrist scholarly perspective, engaging critically with both conservative and liberal scholarship. His approach is measured and judicious.

ashley.name: Timothy R. Ashley
ashley.tradition: Contemporary ·Evangelical OT Scholar· NICOT Series · Numbers Commentary

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Timothy R. Ashley is a Canadian evangelical OT scholar whose contribution to biblical studies is centred on his major commentary on Numbers in the New International Commentary on the Old Testament (NICOT) series, published by Eerdmans in 1993.

The NICOT series represents evangelical scholarship at its most rigorous — combining commitment to the authority of Scripture with full engagement with the Hebrew text, ancient Near Eastern parallels, and the history of interpretation. Ashley’s Numbers is among the series’ most useful volumes for students and preachers: accessible in style but uncompromising in philological depth.

Ashley brings particular strength in Hebrew philology and literary analysis, consistently attending to the structure and narrative artistry of a book that less careful readers dismiss as a mere census list.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Ashley reads Numbers as a unified narrative with careful literary structure, not a miscellaneous collection of laws and lists. He identifies the book’s two census counts (chs.1 and 26) as structural pillars that frame the wilderness generation’s story: counted, failed, and replaced by a new generation counted again on the threshold of the land.

His commentary is particularly strong on the narrative sections — the spy crisis, Korah’s rebellion, Balaam, the Phinehas incident — where he demonstrates that the priestly texts are not liturgical appendices to the Yahwist narrative but integrated theological statements about community, leadership, and holiness.

On the law sections, Ashley provides careful word-study work and attention to the legal logic of the Priestly source without the polemical dismissiveness that sometimes characterises critical scholarship toward the levitical tradition.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Ashley writes from within the conservative evangelical tradition, holding to Mosaic authorship of the Pentateuch while engaging seriously with critical scholarship. His commentary is apologetically responsible without being defensive, and theologically rich without being preachy. It is the standard evangelical-scholarly resource on Numbers.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: The Book of Numbers (NICOT, 1993)His primary work and the source for Ashley notes in this tool. Comprehensive verse-by-verse commentary on the full text of Numbers.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Robert D. Bergen is Professor of Old Testament and Biblical Languages at Hannibal-LaGrange University in Missouri. He earned his PhD from Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary. Before his academic career he served in pastoral ministry.

Bergen's NAC (New American Commentary) volume on 1–2 Samuel (1996) is one of the standard evangelical commentaries on the books. The NAC series is published by Broadman & Holman (now B&H Academic) and is oriented toward pastors, teachers, and serious Bible students within the Southern Baptist tradition and the broader evangelical world.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Bergen reads the Samuel narratives as theological history — carefully crafted accounts that teach theological truths through historical narrative. He is attentive to the text's literary artistry: the contrasts between Saul and David, the narrative function of the Hannah and David psalms, and the way the narrator uses irony and repetition to guide the reader's evaluation of characters and events.

He writes with pastoral clarity, making the text accessible to non-specialists without avoiding difficult historical and textual questions. His treatment of the David narratives is particularly strong on character development and the theological significance of David's failures and repentance.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Bergen writes from within the Southern Baptist evangelical tradition — confessional, inerrantist, and oriented toward the authority and sufficiency of Scripture. His commentary reflects a concern for practical application alongside scholarly analysis. He treats the Samuel narratives as authoritative Scripture with direct relevance for Christian life and leadership.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: 1, 2 Samuel (NAC, 1996) — his major commentary, covering both books in a single volume with attention to narrative theology and pastoral application.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: John Berlin is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. Born in Birmingham, England, he taught at St John’s College, Nottingham before moving to Fuller in 1997.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His three-volume Baker Commentary on Lamentations (2006\u20132008) offers a fresh translation of every psalm with literary and theological commentary. He has also authored major works on Lamentations, Isaiah 40\u201355, and Old Testament theology.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Berlin’s approach is literary-theological: he attends to each psalm’s poetic structure, metaphorical language, and emotional register before drawing theological conclusions. His fresh translations often reveal nuances hidden by familiar renderings.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Daniel Isaac Block is the Gunther H. Knoedler Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Wheaton College, one of the flagship evangelical colleges in North America. Before Wheaton he taught at Southern Baptist Theological Seminary. He earned his doctorate from the University of Liverpool.

Block is a prolific commentator whose work spans Judges, Ruth, Ezekiel, and Deuteronomy. His two-volume NICOT commentary on Ezekiel (1997–1998) is widely regarded as the definitive evangelical commentary on that book. His NAC commentary on Judges and Ruth brought his characteristic blend of philological precision and theological sensitivity to the historical books. More recently, his NIVAC Deuteronomy commentary (2012) reflects his long engagement with the theology of the Torah.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Block combines careful attention to Hebrew grammar and ancient Near Eastern background with a strong theological reading of the text. He is deeply interested in how Old Testament authors understood the covenant relationship between Yahweh and Israel — the obligations, the blessings, the consequences of infidelity.

In Ezekiel, Block excels at tracing the prophet's use of existing biblical traditions and theological vocabulary. He reads Ezekiel's visions not as bizarre apocalyptic imagery but as carefully constructed theological arguments rooted in Israel's worship traditions and covenant theology.

In Judges, Block reads the narratives as theological case studies in covenant failure, showing how each generation's unfaithfulness follows predictable patterns while also introducing new dimensions of degradation. He is attentive to the Deuteronomic theological framework that shapes the book.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Block is a committed evangelical scholar who takes the text's theological claims seriously as divine revelation. He engages substantively with critical scholarship but consistently argues for the coherence and authority of the biblical text. His scholarship is marked by pastoral concern — he writes not merely to inform but to edify believing readers.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The Book of Ezekiel: Chapters 1–24 (NICOT, 1997) — the first volume of his landmark Ezekiel commentary.

The Book of Ezekiel: Chapters 25–48 (NICOT, 1998) — completing the most thorough evangelical treatment of Ezekiel.

Judges, Ruth (NAC, 1999) — combining literary analysis with Deuteronomic-theological interpretation.

Deuteronomy (NIVAC, 2012) — applying his covenant-theology expertise to the Torah's theological centre.

calvin.name: John Calvin
calvin.tradition: 1509–1564 ·Reformer & Theologian· Geneva · Pastor, Theologian, Biblical Commentator

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
John Calvin was born in Noyon, France in 1509 and trained as a lawyer before the Protestant Reformation redirected his intellectual energies toward theology and Scripture. His conversion to Protestantism, which he described as a “sudden conversion,” occurred sometime around 1533. By 1536 he had published the first edition of his Institutes of the Christian Religion — a systematic exposition of Protestant theology that, in its final 1559 form, stands as the greatest work of the Reformation.

After a period in Basel and Strasbourg, Calvin settled in Geneva in 1541, where he spent the rest of his life as the city’s preacher and theological voice. He preached through the entire Bible, often multiple times per week, and from those sermons grew his monumental series of biblical commentaries — covering the entire New Testament except Revelation, and most of the Old Testament.

Calvin died in 1564, having shaped Reformed theology in a way that still defines large portions of Presbyterianism, Congregationalism, and Reformed Baptist traditions worldwide. His commentaries were written in Latin and French and have been continuously in print since the 16th century.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Calvin’s commentaries are distinguished by three principles that set him apart from both his medieval predecessors and many of his Protestant successors. First, brevitas et facilitas — brevity and clarity. Calvin deliberately rejected the encyclopedic method of medieval biblical commentary, which accumulated layers of citation and qualification. He wanted the reader to see the text, not a library about the text.

Second, the “accommodation” principle: God condescends to human capacity in the way Scripture speaks. When Moses describes the firmament or the size of the sun, he is not giving a cosmological treatise but a description suited to ordinary human perception. This hermeneutical move, which Calvin used consistently, anticipated later discussions of genre, phenomenological language, and the relationship between Scripture and natural science.

Third, the unity of the two Testaments. Calvin read the Old Testament not as a religion of works replacing by a religion of grace, but as the same covenant of grace expressed through different forms. Abraham was saved by faith in Christ, just as Paul was — the difference was in the clarity of the revelation, not the substance of the salvation. This gave his Old Testament commentary a christological depth that made him far more attentive to messianic themes than many modern historical-critical commentators.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Calvin is the founder of what became known as the Reformed or Calvinist tradition — a strand of Protestantism defined by its insistence on divine sovereignty in salvation (election, predestination), the supremacy of Scripture, the priesthood of all believers, and the radical sinfulness of human nature apart from grace. The Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), the Heidelberg Catechism (1563), and the Canons of Dort (1619) all represent the tradition Calvin founded, though none are simply identical with his own thought. His commentaries remain living documents in the Reformed community and are increasingly read by evangelicals of all traditions.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Institutes of the Christian Religion (1536–1559)The systematic theological masterwork of the Reformation. Four books covering the knowledge of God, the knowledge of humanity, the way of salvation in Christ, and the external means of grace.

Commentaries on the Pentateuch (1554–1563)His commentary on Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy. The primary source for Calvin notes in this tool. Public domain.

Commentaries on the New Testament (1540–1556)Covering all NT books except Revelation. Still read as models of exegetical commentary — precise, pastoral, and theologically substantial.

Sermons on Genesis (1559–1561)123 sermons preached through Genesis in Geneva. More expansive and pastoral than the commentary, giving insight into how he translated exegesis into proclamation.

sections.body:

catena.name: Catena Aurea
catena.tradition: Compiled c.1263–1268 byThomas Aquinas· Dominican Friar · University of Paris & Naples

sections.title: What Is the Catena Aurea?
sections.body:
TheCatena Aurea(Latin: “Golden Chain”) is a verse-by-verse commentary on all four Gospels compiled by Thomas Aquinas between approximately 1263 and 1268, at the request of Pope Urban IV. It is not itself an original commentary but a carefully edited anthology: a chain of patristic quotations drawn from over eighty Church Fathers and early medieval theologians, woven together into a continuous exposition of each passage.

The Fathers whose voices appear in the Catena include Chrysostom, Augustine, Ambrose, Jerome, Gregory the Great, Cyril of Alexandria, Origen, Hilary of Poitiers, Bede, and many others. Aquinas selected, arranged, and sometimes lightly edited these quotations to create a unified reading of each verse — one in which the reader encounters not one interpreter but the accumulated insight of the first six centuries of Christian biblical interpretation.

The work was translated into English by John Henry Newman in the 1840s (before his conversion to Rome), and this translation is now in the public domain. It remains the most comprehensive collection of patristic commentary on the Gospels available in English.

sections.title: Thomas Aquinas as Compiler
sections.body:
Thomas Aquinas (1225–1274) was the greatest systematic theologian of the medieval period and the intellectual centre of the Dominican order. HisSumma Theologiaerepresents the apex of scholastic theology. But in the Catena Aurea he is not constructing a system — he is curating a tradition. His genius as compiler is in the selection and arrangement: knowing which Father to quote on each verse, how to create dialogue between conflicting interpretations, and when a brief patristic note illuminates more than a lengthy argument.

Aquinas understood the Church Fathers not as authorities to be cited but as readers who had wrestled seriously with the same texts for the same theological stakes. His editorial presence in the Catena is largely invisible — but the intelligence behind the selection is consistently high.

sections.title: What the Catena Adds
sections.body:
The Catena provides something no modern commentary offers: the voice of the early church in its full diversity. Reading Chrysostom on the Sermon on the Mount, then Augustine, then Ambrose — all on the same verse — reveals the range of early Christian interpretation and shows where the tradition agreed and where it diverged. It is the deep past of the text speaking, before the divisions of East and West, Protestant and Catholic, had fully hardened.

For Matthew specifically, the Catena is the richest resource available: Aquinas drew most extensively on Chrysostom’s ninety homilies on Matthew (the most important patristic commentary on Matthew in existence) and Augustine’sDe Sermone Domini in Monte. Reading Catena notes alongside MacArthur, Robertson, and Calvin gives a reader access to what four different centuries of serious Christian scholarship saw in the same passage.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Catena Aurea on the Four Gospels (c.1263–1268)The primary source for Catena notes in this tool. English translation by John Henry Newman (1841–1845), public domain. Covers Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John.

Summa Theologiae (1265–1274)Aquinas’s systematic theological masterwork. Not a biblical commentary but provides context for his interpretive priorities in the Catena.

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body:
Catena Aurea notes apply to Gospel chapters only. Coverage begins with Matthew 1 and will expand as Matthew chapters are built out.

sections.body: John Childs is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. Born in Birmingham, England, he taught at St John’s College, Nottingham before moving to Fuller in 1997.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His three-volume Baker Commentary on Isaiah (2006\u20132008) offers a fresh translation of every psalm with literary and theological commentary. He has also authored major works on Isaiah, Isaiah 40\u201355, and Old Testament theology.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Childs’s approach is literary-theological: he attends to each psalm’s poetic structure, metaphorical language, and emotional register before drawing theological conclusions. His fresh translations often reveal nuances hidden by familiar renderings.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: David Clines is Emeritus Professor of Biblical Studies at the University of Sheffield, one of the most prolific Old Testament scholars of the late 20th century. He is also editor of theDictionary of Classical Hebrew(8 volumes).

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His three-volume WBC Job (1989, 2006, 2011) totals over 2,600 pages — the most comprehensive modern commentary on any biblical book.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Clines reads Job as sophisticated literature that resists systematic theology. He argues that God never actually answers Job’s question, that the epilogue is deliberately ironic, and that the friends are not simply wrong. His approach is philologically rigorous and theologically provocative.

sections.body: John Collins is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. Born in Birmingham, England, he taught at St John’s College, Nottingham before moving to Fuller in 1997.

sections.body: Collins’s approach is literary-theological: he attends to each psalm’s poetic structure, metaphorical language, and emotional register before drawing theological conclusions. His fresh translations often reveal nuances hidden by familiar renderings.

craigie.name: Peter C. Craigie
craigie.tradition: 1938–1985 ·Evangelical OT Scholar· University of Calgary · NICOT Deuteronomy

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Peter Craigie was a British-born OT scholar who taught at the University of Calgary until his death in a car accident in 1985 at age 47. Despite his tragically short career, he produced a body of work whose influence in evangelical OT scholarship has been lasting.

His 1976 NICOT commentary on Deuteronomy remains one of the standard evangelical resources on the book — consistently cited by scholars across traditions for its clarity, learning, and theological depth. He also wrote major works on Psalms (Word Biblical Commentary), ancient Near Eastern warfare, and Ugaritic literature.

Craigie brought to Deuteronomy a combination of deep knowledge of ancient Near Eastern treaty literature, Ugaritic parallels, and the history of interpretation. His engagement with Meredith Kline’s treaty-structure thesis was rigorous and productive, and his treatment of the ethical challenges in Deuteronomy remains one of the most thoughtful in evangelical scholarship.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Craigie reads Deuteronomy through the lens of ancient Near Eastern treaty structure — particularly the Hittite suzerainty treaties of the 14th–13th centuries BC. He argues that Deuteronomy’s six-part structure (preamble, historical prologue, stipulations, document clause, witnesses, blessings/curses) mirrors these treaties and therefore supports an early, Mosaic date for the book.

He consistently attends to the theological dimensions of the legal material, showing how each statute reflects the character of the God who gave it and the community identity of the people who received it. His treatment of theḥērem(ban/devotion-to-destruction) in chs.7 and 13 is particularly nuanced, engaging the ethical challenge with intellectual honesty.

On the rhetorical and homiletical character of Moses’s speeches, Craigie is perceptive about the ways in which covenant renewal language functions differently from straightforward legislation — it is persuasive, pastoral, and personally addressed.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Craigie writes from within the evangelical tradition, holding to Mosaic authorship and the historical reliability of Deuteronomy while engaging seriously with critical scholarship. His commentaries are notable for their intellectual honesty about difficult texts and their refusal to shelter behind easy answers.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The Book of Deuteronomy (NICOT, 1976)The primary source for Craigie notes in this tool. Verse-by-verse commentary with strong ANE comparative work and theological depth.

Psalms 1–50 (Word Biblical Commentary, 1983)His other major commentary, showing the same combination of linguistic rigour and theological sensitivity applied to the Psalter.

The Problem of War in the Old Testament (1978)A monograph wrestling with the ethical dimensions of OT warfare — directly relevant to Deuteronomy’sḥēremlegislation.

sections.body:
Craigie notes appear throughout Deuteronomy in this tool.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Michael V. Fox is the Jay C. and Ruth Halls Bascom Professor Emeritus of Hebrew at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. He is one of the leading Hebraists of his generation, with expertise spanning wisdom literature, Egyptian literature, and Semitic philology.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: HisA Time to Tear Down and a Time to Build Up(1999) and the JPS commentary on Qohelet (2004) together constitute the most comprehensive philological analysis of Ecclesiastes in English.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Fox’s signature contribution is the argument thathebelmeans “absurd” rather than “meaningless” or “vain.” Life is not empty but irrational — it defies the human expectation that effort should produce proportionate results. This reading transforms Ecclesiastes from nihilism into existential protest, closer in spirit to Camus than to despair.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Duane Garrett is John R. Sampey Professor of Old Testament Interpretation at The Southern Baptist Theological Seminary in Louisville, Kentucky. He has authored commentaries on Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Song of Songs, as well as studies in Hosea and Joel.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His NAC Song of Songs (2004) reads the poem as a unified dramatic sequence celebrating marital love. He also contributed the NAC Proverbs volume and has written on OT introduction and Hebrew exegesis.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Garrett reads Song of Songs as a single poem with a dramatic arc, not a loose anthology. He identifies the speakers (Beloved, Lover, Daughters of Jerusalem) and traces their relationship through courtship, wedding, and consummation. His approach is literary-evangelical: the text is both great love poetry and a theological statement about the goodness of marital intimacy as God designed it.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: John Goldingay is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. Born in Birmingham, England, he taught at St John’s College, Nottingham before moving to Fuller in 1997.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His three-volume Baker Commentary on Psalms (2006\u20132008) offers a fresh translation of every psalm with literary and theological commentary. He has also authored major works on Daniel, Isaiah 40\u201355, and Old Testament theology.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Goldingay’s approach is literary-theological: he attends to each psalm’s poetic structure, metaphorical language, and emotional register before drawing theological conclusions. His fresh translations often reveal nuances hidden by familiar renderings.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Norman Habel is an Australian Lutheran theologian and biblical scholar known for pioneering eco-theology and the Earth Bible project.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His Old Testament Library commentary on Job (1985) remains a standard academic reference.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Habel’s key insight is that Job is structured as a legal drama. Job functions as plaintiff, God as defendant, the friends as expert witnesses. The legal vocabulary throughout — witness, arbiter, redeemer, oath, verdict — supports this reading.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Richard S. Hess is Distinguished Professor of Old Testament and Semitic Languages at Denver Seminary in Littleton, Colorado. He earned his PhD from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, giving him deep roots in Israeli biblical scholarship and Semitic epigraphy.

Hess's TOTC (Tyndale Old Testament Commentary) volume on Joshua (1996) is one of the most widely used shorter evangelical commentaries on the book. He has also published extensively on ancient Near Eastern backgrounds to the Bible, including 'Israelite Religions: An Archaeological and Biblical Survey' (2007) and studies on personal names in the Amarna letters and other ancient texts.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Hess excels at situating the biblical text within its ancient Near Eastern context. His Joshua commentary consistently draws on archaeological evidence, Late Bronze Age geopolitics, and comparative Near Eastern treaty and conquest literature to illuminate the narrative.

He pays careful attention to personal names, place names, and geographical details — the kind of concrete textual data that anchors the narrative in historical reality. His work on the Amarna letters and other extra-biblical sources gives him particular authority on Canaanite culture and religion.

While his TOTC commentary is concise by design, it packs a remarkable density of historical and textual insight into its compact format.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Hess is an evangelical scholar who combines confessional commitment with serious academic engagement. His Jerusalem training and his involvement in international biblical archaeology give his work a breadth that bridges confessional and academic worlds. He argues for the historical reliability of the biblical narratives while engaging honestly with archaeological evidence that complicates simple reconstructions.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Joshua (TOTC, 1996) — a concise but historically rich commentary on the conquest narratives.

Israelite Religions: An Archaeological and Biblical Survey (2007) — a comprehensive study of Israel's religious practices in their ancient Near Eastern context.

Studies in the Personal Names of Genesis 1–11 (1993) — exemplifying his expertise in onomastics and comparative Semitic studies.

sections.title: Appears In

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
David M. Howard Jr. is Professor of Old Testament at Bethel Seminary in St. Paul, Minnesota. He previously taught at Trinity Evangelical Divinity School and New Orleans Baptist Theological Seminary. He earned his PhD from the University of Michigan.

Howard is best known for his NAC (New American Commentary) volume on Joshua (1998), which remains one of the standard evangelical commentaries on the book. He has also written extensively on the Psalms, including 'The Structure of Psalms 93–100' (1997), and has contributed to numerous edited volumes on Old Testament history and theology. He served as president of the Evangelical Theological Society.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Howard reads Joshua as a theological narrative about God's faithfulness in fulfilling the land promises made to the patriarchs. He pays careful attention to the book's literary structure — the movement from conquest narratives to tribal allotments to covenant renewal — and reads each section as contributing to a unified theological argument.

He is attentive to geographical and historical detail, drawing on archaeological evidence where it illuminates the text. He engages with critical questions about the historicity of the conquest while maintaining that the text presents a reliable account of Israel's entry into the land.

Howard writes with clarity and precision, making his commentary accessible to pastors and students without sacrificing academic rigour.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Howard is a confessional evangelical who treats the biblical text as inspired and authoritative. He engages with the full range of critical scholarship but reads the text within the framework of evangelical faith. His work reflects a concern for the text's relevance to believing communities — not merely its historical background or literary features.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Joshua (NAC, 1998) — his major commentary, combining historical, literary, and theological analysis of the conquest narratives.

The Structure of Psalms 93–100 (1997) — a study in the literary and theological coherence of the enthronement psalms.

An Introduction to the Old Testament Historical Books (1993) — a widely used evangelical textbook.

hubbard.name: Robert L. Hubbard Jr.
hubbard.tradition: 1943– ·Evangelical Biblical Scholar· North Park Theological Seminary · NICOT Commentary on Ruth

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Robert L. Hubbard Jr. is an American evangelical biblical scholar who taught Old Testament and Hebrew at North Park Theological Seminary in Chicago for many years. His 1988 commentary on Ruth in the New International Commentary on the Old Testament (NICOT) series is widely regarded as the definitive evangelical-scholarly treatment of the book — thorough in its philological analysis, careful in its engagement with ancient Near Eastern legal customs, and consistently attentive to the book’s theological dimensions.

Hubbard brings to Ruth the same combination of linguistic precision and theological sensitivity that characterises the best of the NICOT series. He engages the major critical and interpretive debates — the date and provenance of the book, the nature of the go’el institution, the relationship between the sandal ceremony and levirate law — while never losing sight of the narrative as a story about real human beings exercising covenant faithfulness.

Beyond Ruth, Hubbard has contributed to Old Testament studies through his work on the redemption motif across the Hebrew canon and his involvement in the New International Commentary series as a whole.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Hubbard reads Ruth as a carefully crafted literary unit whose artistry serves its theology. He is attentive to the book’s narrative structure, its patterns of repetition and contrast (fullness and emptiness, loyalty and self-interest, insider and outsider), and its rich deployment of the hesed motif as both theme and characterising virtue.

His philological work on key terms — go’el (kinsman-redeemer), hesed (covenant loyalty), and the sandal-removal ceremony — is foundational for any serious study of Ruth. He situates these terms within their ancient Near Eastern legal and social contexts while drawing out their theological significance within the OT canon.

Hubbard is particularly strong on the typological dimensions of the text: Boaz as a type of the divine redeemer, the hesed of Ruth as a mirror of the hesed of YHWH, and the genealogy’s placement of the story within the Davidic—and ultimately messianic—line.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body:
Hubbard writes from within the evangelical Protestant tradition, with commitments to the authority and coherence of the biblical text, historical-grammatical exegesis, and theological interpretation that situates Ruth within the larger biblical-theological narrative. He engages critical scholarship seriously without being governed by its methodological presuppositions.

His treatment of Ruth’s Gentile status and her full inclusion in the covenant community is particularly sensitive — he reads the book not as an ethnic polemic but as a narrative demonstration that hesed is a covenant quality available to anyone willing to live by it.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The Book of Ruth (NICOT, 1988)The definitive evangelical commentary on Ruth. Combines thorough philological analysis of Hebrew terms with deep engagement with the book’s narrative art and theological themes. The primary source for Hubbard notes in this tool.

The Book of Ruth — Supplementary LiteratureHubbard’s work on the go’el institution and the redemption motif in the OT has appeared in JETS and other evangelical journals, extending the NICOT commentary’s legal-historical analysis.

sections.body:

japhet.name: Sara Japhet
japhet.tradition: Professor of Bible, Hebrew University of Jerusalem

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Sara Japhet (1934–2022) was Professor of Bible at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem and one of the most distinguished biblical scholars of the 20th century. Born in Czechoslovakia and educated in Israel, she became the world's foremost authority on the books of Chronicles, devoting her career to demonstrating the Chronicler's literary artistry and theological independence.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: Her magisterial commentary on I & II Chronicles in the Old Testament Library (OTL) series (1993) is considered the definitive critical treatment. Her earlier monographThe Ideology of the Book of Chronicles and Its Place in Biblical Thought(1977/1989) established the framework that all subsequent Chronicles scholarship has engaged. She argued convincingly that the Chronicler was not merely copying Samuel-Kings but creating an independent theological work with its own sources and purposes.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Japhet is meticulous on textual detail, source analysis, and the Chronicler's editorial technique. She demonstrates how the Chronicler reshaped inherited traditions to address the concerns of the post-exilic community: identity, legitimate worship, and the continuity of God's promises. Her commentary is essential for understanding the differences between Chronicles and Samuel-Kings — not as errors but as deliberate theological choices.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Karen Jobes is Gerald F. Hawthorne Professor Emerita of New Testament Greek and Exegesis at Wheaton College. Though primarily a New Testament scholar, her NIVAC commentary on Esther (1999) is widely regarded as one of the finest evangelical treatments of the book. She brings both rigorous historical method and pastoral sensitivity to a text that raises difficult theological questions.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: Her NIVAC Esther commentary excels at three levels: original meaning (Persian-period context, literary structure, the absence of God's name), bridging contexts (how the book functioned in Jewish and Christian communities), and contemporary significance (providence, identity, courage under threat). She also authored major works on 1 Peter and the Septuagint.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Jobes reads Esther as a theology of providence without theophany. God is never named but never absent. She traces the "coincidences" that drive the plot — the king's insomnia, the timing of the banquets, Mordecai's unrewarded service — as evidence of invisible divine orchestration. Her approach is literary-theological: the story's artistry is itself the argument for God's presence.

keener.name: Craig S. Keener
keener.tradition: 1960– ·NT Scholar· Asbury Theological Seminary · Acts Commentary

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Craig Keener is Professor of Biblical Studies at Asbury Theological Seminary and one of the most prolific NT scholars of his generation. He has written commentaries on Matthew, John, Romans, Galatians, 1–2 Corinthians, Revelation, and his monumental four-volume Acts (Baker Academic, 2012–2015).

Keener is best known for his socio-historical method — bringing the full weight of Greco-Roman literary, social, and cultural background to illuminate NT texts. His commentaries are famously encyclopedic: the Acts set runs to nearly 5,000 pages and cites over 30,000 ancient sources.

He also wrote a landmark academic study,Miracles: The Credibility of the New Testament Accounts(Baker Academic, 2011), defending the historicity of miracle claims through cross-cultural and historical evidence.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Keener’s primary interpretive tool is ancient context. He believes that the NT writers were sophisticated authors communicating to culturally embedded audiences, and that recovering that original context illuminates meaning that modern readers otherwise miss.

On Acts, he consistently demonstrates that Luke’s historical claims are both plausible and historically coherent against the backdrop of first-century Roman administration, Jewish diaspora practice, and Greco-Roman social conventions. He reads Acts as genuine historiography in the ancient sense — selective but not fabricated, rhetorical but not fictional.

His handling of the Pentecost narrative, Paul’s missionary journeys, and the trial narratives is particularly illuminating, drawing on papyri, inscriptions, and literary sources to establish the verisimilitude of Luke’s account.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Keener is a committed evangelical with a heart for cross-cultural mission. His scholarship is in service of the church, not merely the academy. He holds to the historical reliability of Acts and the NT broadly while engaging seriously with critical scholarship and the full range of ancient evidence.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Acts: An Exegetical Commentary, 4 vols. (Baker Academic, 2012–2015)The primary source for Keener notes in this tool. The most comprehensive evangelical commentary on Acts ever produced.

The Gospel of John: A Commentary, 2 vols. (Hendrickson, 2003)Equally comprehensive treatment of the Fourth Gospel.

A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Eerdmans, 1999)His earlier major NT commentary, still widely used.

kidner.name: Derek Kidner
kidner.tradition: Old Testament Scholar & Master of Concision

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Derek Kidner (1913–2008) was a British Old Testament scholar and Warden of Tyndale House, Cambridge, one of the world's leading centres for biblical research. Ordained in the Church of England, he combined pastoral sensitivity with rigorous scholarship, producing commentaries that are models of economy and insight.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His Tyndale Old Testament Commentaries on Genesis (1967), Proverbs (1964), Psalms (1973–75), and Ezra & Nehemiah (1979) remain widely used decades after publication. Each is characterised by remarkable compression: Kidner could illuminate an entire passage in a single sentence where others required paragraphs. His Ezra-Nehemiah commentary is particularly valued for its theological clarity on the tension between exclusivism and universalism in the post-exilic community.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Kidner wrote with a literary ear and a pastor's heart. He treats biblical texts as coherent theological literature, not source-critical puzzles. His gift was the pithy observation — the one sentence that reframes a passage entirely. On Ezra's prayer of confession he writes: "Not a word of excuse; not a word of self-pity. This is repentance that has shed every trace of self-regard." His commentaries reward re-reading; new layers emerge with each encounter.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Jon Levenson is Albert A. List Professor of Jewish Studies at Harvard Divinity School, one of the most distinguished positions in Jewish biblical scholarship. A fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, he has written extensively on the theology of the Hebrew Bible, creation, death and resurrection in ancient Israel, and Jewish-Christian relations.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His Old Testament Library commentary on Esther (1997) is the standard academic reference. He is also author ofThe Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son(1993),Creation and the Persistence of Evil(1988), andSinai and Zion(1985) — each a landmark in biblical theology. His work consistently illuminates the theological depth of texts that others treat as merely historical.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Levenson reads Esther through the lens of the Exodus: Haman is the new Pharaoh who seeks genocide; Esther is the new Moses who intercedes; the Jews' victory echoes the Sea crossing. He is attentive to Purim traditions and to the book's role in Jewish liturgical life. His commentary combines critical rigor with genuine appreciation for the text's theological claims — a rare combination in academic scholarship.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Tremper Longman III is Distinguished Scholar and Professor Emeritus of Biblical Studies at Westmont College. He has authored or co-authored over thirty books, including commentaries, introductions, and theological studies across the Old Testament.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His NICOT Ecclesiastes (1998) and NICOT Song of Solomon (2001) are standard evangelical commentaries. He also co-authoredAn Introduction to the Old Testamentwith Raymond Dillard, one of the most widely used OT survey texts.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Longman reads Qoheleth as a character within the book whose pessimistic observations are framed and corrected by the epilogue narrator (12:9–14). The “fear God and keep his commandments” conclusion recontextualises everything Qoheleth said. His approach is literary-canonical: the final form of the text, not just Qoheleth’s voice, carries the theological message.

macarthur.name: John MacArthur
macarthur.tradition: b. 1939 ·Conservative Evangelical· Pastor-Scholar · Grace Community Church, Sun Valley CA

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
John MacArthur was born in Los Angeles in 1939, the fourth generation of a pastoral family. He studied at Talbot Theological Seminary, where he was shaped by the grammatical-historical method of biblical interpretation — the conviction that the plain meaning of the text, read in its original linguistic and historical context, is the authoritative foundation for all theology and preaching.

Since 1969 he has served as pastor-teacher of Grace Community Church in Sun Valley, California, where his expository preaching through entire books of the Bible has shaped a generation of evangelical ministry. His radio broadcast “Grace to You” reaches millions worldwide. He founded The Master’s University and The Master’s Seminary, institutions committed to his convictions about biblical inerrancy and careful exegesis.

MacArthur belongs to the Reformed Baptist tradition, holding to the doctrines of grace (election, effectual calling, perseverance of the saints) while rejecting infant baptism and continuationism. His exegetical output is remarkable in its consistency and volume: over fifty years of consecutive expository preaching through the New Testament and significant portions of the Old Testament.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
MacArthur’s method is grammatical-historical exposition: identify the author’s intended meaning through careful attention to grammar, syntax, original language, and historical context, then apply that meaning to the contemporary reader. He is deeply suspicious of allegorical or spiritualising interpretations that bypass the text’s plain sense.

What makes MacArthur distinctive is his insistence on the theological weight of every passage. He consistently asks: what does this text teach about God, about human nature, about sin, about salvation? His commentary is not merely descriptive but confessional — he reads Scripture as a unified revelation of the God who saves. This gives his notes a doctrinal density that is sometimes absent from more academic commentaries.

His weaknesses are the predictable ones: he is less attentive to the literary artistry of the text than literary scholars, thinner on ancient Near Eastern background than Jewish scholars, and his application framework is resolutely evangelical-pastoral rather than historical-critical. But within his own method he is consistently rigorous and pastorally warm.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: MacArthur stands in the line of Protestant scholasticism as mediated through 19th-century Princeton theology (Hodge, Warfield) and 20th-century fundamentalism. He holds to plenary verbal inspiration and inerrancy of Scripture, the five solas of the Reformation, and a dispensational premillennial eschatology. He is perhaps the most influential expository preacher in the English-speaking evangelical world of the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The MacArthur Study Bible (1997)Over 25,000 study notes covering the entire Bible. The primary source for notes in this tool. Notes are faithful paraphrases, not verbatim quotations.

The MacArthur New Testament Commentary (33 vols.)Verse-by-verse exposition of every NT book, developed from decades of expository preaching at Grace Community Church.

The Gospel According to Jesus (1988)His landmark treatment of Lordship salvation — the controversial argument that saving faith necessarily involves submission to Christ as Lord.

Charismatic Chaos (1992)His cessationist critique of the charismatic movement, arguing that miraculous gifts ceased with the apostolic era.

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body:
MacArthur notes appear in every chapter section across all five books currently in the tool.

Genesis 1Exodus 1Proverbs 1Matthew 1

marcus.name: Joel Marcus
marcus.tradition: b. 1951 ·NT Scholar · Anchor Bible Commentator· Duke Divinity School

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Joel Marcus is Professor of New Testament and Christian Origins at Duke Divinity School, where he has taught for over two decades. He studied at Columbia University and Union Theological Seminary, and his academic formation brought him into deep engagement with both Christian and Jewish interpretive traditions — a dual inheritance that shapes every page of his Markan commentary.

His two-volume Anchor Bible commentary on Mark —Mark 1–8(2000) andMark 8–16(2009) — is widely regarded as the most comprehensive critical commentary on the Second Gospel in English. It synthesises over a century of Markan scholarship while making genuinely original contributions, particularly in the areas of Jewish background, Qumran parallels, Roman imperial context, and the Gospel’s use of the Old Testament.

Marcus brings to the text a rare combination: technical precision in Greek and Hebrew, encyclopaedic knowledge of Second Temple Judaism and its texts (Josephus, Philo, the Dead Sea Scrolls, Pseudepigrapha), and a keen sensitivity to the theological weight of Mark’s narrative. His commentary is the standard first port of call for serious Markan scholarship.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Marcus works in the tradition of redaction criticism and historical-critical scholarship, but he is not reductively so. His primary question at each passage is: what does this text mean in its historical and literary context? That means attending simultaneously to the Greek text, the Old Testament allusions (in both Hebrew and LXX forms), the Jewish background, the Graeco-Roman social world, and the literary structure Mark has given his material.

His treatment of Jewish background is particularly distinctive. Where many NT commentators treat Second Temple Judaism as context rather than dialogue partner, Marcus reads Mark in genuine conversation with it — showing how the Qumran community applied Isaiah 40:3 to their own mission, how Markan exorcism language maps onto Jewish demonology, how the passion predictions echo specific Servant Song texts. The result is a portrait of a Gospel far more embedded in its Jewish matrix than earlier readings acknowledged.

Marcus is also sensitive to the Roman imperial context. His reading of “Legion” (5:9), the feeding of five thousand as a counter-imperial banquet, and the trial before Pilate as a collision of two kingdoms gives Mark a political edge that purely spiritual readings miss. Jesus’s proclamation of the kingdom is heard, on Marcus’s reading, as a direct challenge to Caesar’s claim.

His approach to Mark’s theology is that of a respectful but critical historian: he believes Mark is making genuine theological claims that deserve to be taken seriously, even when he cannot personally endorse them from a confessional standpoint. This makes his commentary unusually useful across a wide range of readers.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body:
Marcus writes from within the mainstream of historical-critical NT scholarship. He is Jewish by background and reads the New Testament with a deep appreciation for its Jewish roots — but also with the critical distance of a scholar who does not approach the text as authoritative Scripture in the evangelical sense. This is precisely what makes him so valuable alongside commentators like MacArthur or Calvin: he asks questions that confessional commentators rarely do, and he hears Jewish resonances that Christian commentators often miss.

His work represents the best of what historical-critical scholarship offers: rigorous, honest, learned, and genuinely illuminating — without the reductionism that sometimes afflicts the tradition. He takes Mark’s theology seriously as theology, even while analysing its historical genesis.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Mark 1–8, Anchor Bible Vol. 27 (2000)The first volume of his magnum opus. Comprehensive introduction to Mark’s authorship, date, sources, theology, and the text of the first eight chapters. Indispensable for serious Markan study.

Mark 8–16, Anchor Bible Vol. 27A (2009)Completes the commentary with the second half of Mark, including the most detailed treatment of the Passion narrative in any English-language Markan commentary.

The Way of the Lord: Christological Exegesis of the Old Testament in the Gospel of Mark (1992)Marcus’s doctoral research. Demonstrates how Mark systematically deploys OT texts to define Jesus’s identity, particularly through the new-exodus and Servant Song traditions.

Mark — A Short Commentary (2020)A more accessible one-volume commentary distilling his major work for students and general readers, without the full scholarly apparatus.

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body:
Marcus notes apply to the Gospel of Mark only. Full coverage across all 16 chapters.

milgrom.name: Jacob Milgrom
milgrom.tradition: 1923–2010 ·Jewish Biblical Scholar· UC Berkeley · Anchor Bible Leviticus & Numbers

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Jacob Milgrom was born in New York in 1923 and became one of the most prolific and influential scholars of the Hebrew Bible in the twentieth century. He taught for decades at the University of California, Berkeley, where he shaped generations of students in OT studies.

His magnum opus is the three-volume Anchor Bible Commentary on Leviticus (1991, 1999, 2001) — at over 2,700 pages the most comprehensive critical commentary on any biblical book in English. He also wrote the Anchor Bible Numbers (1990), extending the same rigorous method to the wilderness narratives.

Milgrom brought to the priestly texts a combination of deep knowledge of rabbinic and Second Temple literature, Ancient Near Eastern comparative religion, and rigorous philological method. Where earlier scholars had dismissed the levitical legislation as arid ritualism, Milgrom demonstrated that it encodes a coherent theology of holiness, pollution, and moral responsibility.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Milgrom’s central contribution is his interpretation of the priestly purity system as a moral theology. In his reading, the major sources of ritual impurity in Leviticus — death, disease, genital discharge — all represent forces hostile to life. The tabernacle/temple is a sanctuary of life, and impurity pollutes it not through physical contamination but through moral-symbolic associations with the powers of death.

He argues that the sin offering (ḥaṭṭāʾt) functions not primarily to atone for the offerer but to purge the sanctuary itself from the impurity caused by Israel’s sins. This reconceptualisation of the sacrificial system has been enormously influential and remains debated.

On Numbers, Milgrom consistently illuminates the Priestly source’s literary artistry and theological intentions, showing how the wilderness narratives function as a theology of community, leadership, and covenant failure.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Milgrom writes from within the Jewish scholarly tradition, combining the philological rigour of the academic guild with deep familiarity with the Talmudic and medieval Jewish interpretive heritage. He does not read the priestly texts as Christian allegory but as documents of a living religious system whose logic and beauty deserve to be understood on their own terms.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Leviticus 1–16 (Anchor Bible, 1991)The first and most influential volume — over 1,100 pages covering the sacrificial system and purity laws with unparalleled depth.

Leviticus 17–22 (Anchor Bible, 1999)The Holiness Code, arguing that its ethics anticipate and extend the priestly purity system into the moral domain.

Leviticus 23–27 (Anchor Bible, 2001)The festival calendar, jubilee, and concluding legislation.

Numbers (Anchor Bible, 1990)A comprehensive commentary on the wilderness narratives combining literary, historical, and theological analysis.

sections.body:
Milgrom notes appear throughout Leviticus and Numbers in this tool, covering the priestly legislation, purity laws, and wilderness narratives.

netbible.name: NET Bible Notes
netbible.tradition: First published 2001, revised 2019 ·Biblical Studies Press· Dallas, Texas · Over 60,000 translator notes

sections.title: What Are the NET Bible Notes?
sections.body:
The NET Bible (New English Translation) was produced by a team of more than twenty-five biblical scholars associated with Dallas Theological Seminary and is unique among modern translations in the scale and transparency of its annotation apparatus. TheFull Notes Editioncontains over 60,000 translator notes that document the textual, translational, and exegetical decisions behind every verse of the translation.

These notes fall into three categories:textual notesdocument manuscript evidence and explain why one reading was chosen over another;translational notesexplain why a Hebrew or Greek phrase was rendered a particular way and what alternative renderings are possible; andexegetical notesprovide background information, cross-references, and interpretive context needed to understand a passage.

The result is something no other widely-used Bible translation provides: a publicly visible record of the scholarly reasoning behind every translational decision. The NET Bible notes make the translation process transparent in a way that reveals how much interpretive work is embedded in every English rendering of the original text.

sections.title: Why NET Notes in This Tool?
sections.body:
Most study Bible commentaries (including MacArthur’s) focus on what a passage means theologically. The NET notes focus on a prior question: what does the original text actually say, and how certain are we about that? They document manuscript variants, discuss the semantic range of Hebrew and Greek words, and flag where translators made judgment calls that other scholars might make differently.

This makes NET notes an ideal complement to theological commentary. When MacArthur says “this verse teaches X”, the NET note often reveals whether “this verse” is itself a matter of textual uncertainty, or whether the translation he is commenting on obscures an ambiguity present in the original. Having this critical layer visible makes the tool more honest about the interpretive process.

The NET Bible notes are available under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0), which allows free use with attribution. Notes in this tool are faithful paraphrases with attribution, not verbatim quotations.

sections.title: Scope and Limitation
sections.body: The NET notes are strongest on textual criticism and translational transparency. They are not devotional, not pastoral, and not primarily theological — they are the annotations of a translation team explaining their work. They do not have the literary sensitivity of Alter, the Jewish theological depth of Sarna, or the doctrinal richness of Calvin. But they provide a critical baseline that all other commentary in this tool rests on: a clear account of what the Hebrew and Greek actually say, and where we are and are not certain about it.

sections.title: Key Publication
sections.body: NET Bible Full Notes Edition (2001, revised 2019)The primary source for NET notes in this tool. Over 60,000 translator notes covering the entire Bible. Available at netbible.org under CC BY 4.0. Notes are paraphrased with attribution.

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body:
NET Bible notes will appear across all books and chapters, focusing on verses where translational choices significantly affect meaning or where textual uncertainty is significant.

Genesis 1

oconnor.name: John O'Connor
oconnor.tradition: Tears of the World (3 vols) & Fresh Translation

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: John O'Connor is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. Born in Birmingham, England, he taught at St John’s College, Nottingham before moving to Fuller in 1997.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His three-volume Baker Commentary on Lamentations (2006\u20132008) offers a fresh translation of every psalm with literary and theological commentary. He has also authored major works on Lamentations, Isaiah 40\u201355, and Old Testament theology.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: O'Connor’s approach is literary-theological: he attends to each psalm’s poetic structure, metaphorical language, and emotional register before drawing theological conclusions. His fresh translations often reveal nuances hidden by familiar renderings.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: John Oswalt is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. Born in Birmingham, England, he taught at St John’s College, Nottingham before moving to Fuller in 1997.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His three-volume Baker Commentary on Isaiah (2006\u20132008) offers a fresh translation of every psalm with literary and theological commentary. He has also authored major works on Isaiah, Isaiah 40\u201355, and Old Testament theology.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Oswalt’s approach is literary-theological: he attends to each psalm’s poetic structure, metaphorical language, and emotional register before drawing theological conclusions. His fresh translations often reveal nuances hidden by familiar renderings.

provan.name: A.A. Anderson
provan.tradition: 1927–2009 ·OT Scholar· University of Manchester · WBC 2 Samuel

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Iain William Provan is Marshall Sheppard Professor of Biblical Studies at Regent College, Vancouver. A Scottish-born Old Testament scholar, he previously held chairs at the University of Edinburgh. Provan is known for combining rigorous historical-critical scholarship with a deep commitment to reading Scripture as canonical literature that addresses the contemporary church.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body: Provan reads 1–2 Kings as a theological narrative rather than merely a historical chronicle. His NIBCOT commentary focuses on the text’s literary artistry — narrative pacing, characterization, irony, and structural design — while always asking what the Deuteronomistic Historian intended the reader to learn theologically. He is particularly strong on the theological evaluation of kings and the prophetic word as the engine that drives the narrative.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Provan writes from a Reformed evangelical perspective with particular interest in the philosophy of history. He has argued vigorously (inA Biblical History of Israel, co-authored with Long and Longman) that the biblical narratives should be treated as credible historical testimony rather than dismissed as late theological construction.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: 1 and 2 Kings(New International Biblical Commentary OT, 1995) — Literary-theological commentary with attention to narrative structure and Deuteronomistic themes. Also:A Biblical History of Israel(2003, with Long and Longman).

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body: Provan notes appear throughout 1 Kings and 2 Kings.

rhoads.name: Rhoads & Michie
rhoads.tradition: David Rhoads (b. 1941) & Donald Michie ·Narrative Critics · Authors ofMark as Story

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
David Rhoads is Professor Emeritus of New Testament at the Lutheran School of Theology at Chicago. Donald Michie is a literary scholar whose collaboration with Rhoads produced one of the most consequential works in twentieth-century Gospel studies. Their 1982 bookMark as Story: An Introduction to the Narrative of a Gospelfounded the discipline of Markan narrative criticism and permanently changed how scholars and readers approach the Second Gospel.

The book grew out of Rhoads’s conviction that Mark deserved to be read as a unified literary work — not merely as a collection of pericopes to be dissected by form criticism or a quarry for reconstructing the historical Jesus, but as a carefully crafted narrative with its own narrator, characters, plot, and rhetoric. Michie’s background in literary studies provided the theoretical framework; Rhoads provided the biblical scholarship. The result was a genuinely interdisciplinary work that brought the tools of narrative theory to bear on a Gospel text for the first time in a sustained way.

A second edition appeared in 1999, joined by Joanna Dewey; the third edition (2012) revised the analysis in light of three decades of subsequent scholarship and includes expanded discussion of the narrator’s rhetoric and the story’s ideological perspective. The three editions together trace the maturation of narrative criticism as a discipline.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Rhoads and Michie’s method is narrative criticism: the close reading of the text as a narrative, attending to the techniques by which it produces meaning. Their framework analyses the text along three axes. First, thenarrator— what kind of narrator does Mark employ? What does the narrator know (everything), what does the narrator reveal (selectively), and how does the narrator position the reader in relation to the characters and events? Second,characters— how are Jesus, the disciples, the authorities, and minor characters constructed through action, speech, and the narrator’s commentary? Third,plot— how does Mark structure his narrative, what tensions drive it forward, and how does it resolve (or refuse to resolve)?

Several of their analytical concepts have become foundational in Markan studies. Thereliable narrator: Mark’s narrator is omniscient and trustworthy, and the reader who trusts the narrator’s opening declaration (1:1) is positioned to understand what characters within the story cannot.Intercalation(the Markan sandwich): the technique of inserting one story inside another so that each interprets the other.Dramatic irony: the reader knows what characters do not. Thedisciples’ characterization arc: the disciples begin with positive characterization (immediate obedience, privileged insider access) and progressively harden into incomprehension and failure.

Their approach reads Mark on its own terms rather than treating it as a window onto historical events behind the text. This is methodologically distinct from historical criticism (Marcus) and from theological exposition (MacArthur, Calvin). Together, the three perspectives give a stereoscopic depth to any Markan passage: the historical context, the narrative construction, and the theological import.

sections.title: Significance
sections.body:
BeforeMark as Story, the standard critical assumption was that Mark was the least literary of the Gospels — a rough, unpolished compilation of early tradition by an author with minimal literary skill. Rhoads and Michie’s analysis reversed this judgment. Mark, they demonstrated, is a carefully constructed narrative, and what had been read as clumsiness was often deliberate technique: abrupt transitions as urgency, the withholding of interiority as characterization by action, repetition as structural scaffolding.

Their work also restored attention to the reader’s experience of the text. Historical criticism asked where the text came from; narrative criticism asked what it does — how it shapes the reader’s knowledge, sympathy, and understanding as the story unfolds. This shift of focus from origin to effect produced a new generation of Markan scholarship, of which the third edition ofMark as Storystands as both monument and continuing resource.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Mark as Story: An Introduction to the Narrative of a Gospel, 3rd ed. (Rhoads, Dewey & Michie, 2012)The primary source for Rhoads notes in this tool. The foundational text of Markan narrative criticism, now in its third edition. Analyses narrator, characters, and plot in systematic detail. Essential reading for anyone who wants to understand how Mark works as a story.

Reading Mark, Engaging the Gospel (David Rhoads, 2004)A collection of Rhoads’s essays developing and extending the narrative-critical approach in specific Markan passages and themes, including his work on the Syrophoenician woman and the nature of Markan characterization.

The Challenge of Diversity (David Rhoads, 1996)Extends narrative-critical methods to the question of community and identity in early Christianity, showing how the narrative approach illuminates not just the text but its social world.

sections.body:
Rhoads notes apply to the Gospel of Mark only. Full coverage across all 16 chapters.

robertson.name: A.T. Robertson
robertson.tradition: 1863–1934 ·NT Greek Scholar· Southern Baptist Theological Seminary · Louisville, Kentucky

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Archibald Thomas Robertson was born in Cherryville, North Carolina in 1863 and spent virtually his entire academic career at Southern Baptist Theological Seminary in Louisville, Kentucky, where he taught New Testament Greek from 1888 until his death in 1934. He was arguably the greatest Greek grammarian in the history of American biblical scholarship.

His 1914 work,A Grammar of the Greek New Testament in the Light of Historical Research, runs to 1,454 pages and remains a standard reference work over a century later. It synthesised decades of advances in papyrology, koine Greek studies, and comparative linguistics into a comprehensive account of the Greek of the New Testament — demonstrating that it was not a corrupted form of classical Greek but the living vernacular of the Hellenistic Mediterranean world.

His six-volumeWord Pictures in the New Testament(1930-1933), completed just before his death, brought that grammatical expertise to a wider audience: a verse-by-verse commentary through every New Testament book focused entirely on what the Greek is doing. It is now in the public domain and represents the most sustained grammatical commentary on the NT in English.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Robertson’s approach is resolutely grammatical: his interest at every verse is the Greek itself — verb tenses, case functions, particle usage, wordplay, the difference between aorist and perfect, between indicative and subjunctive. He is not primarily interested in theology, application, or background (though he engages all three) — he wants the reader to see what the Greek is doing.

This makes Robertson an invaluable complement to commentators who focus on theology or background. Where MacArthur asks “what does this verse mean for Christian life?” and Sarna asks “how does this text compare to its ANE environment?”, Robertson asks “what does this Greek word or construction actually say?” His notes on Greek tense and case are often the most concise and accurate available in English.

He is strongest in the Pauline letters, Acts, and the Gospel of John, where the Greek is most complex and his grammatical precision yields the most insight. His notes are denser and more technically demanding than other commentators in this tool — but readers who engage with them gain direct access to what the original language is doing in ways no translation fully conveys.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Robertson was a conservative Southern Baptist who held to biblical inerrancy and evangelical orthodoxy. But his commentaries are less confessionally driven than those of MacArthur or Calvin — his primary interest is grammatical precision, not doctrinal exposition. He is one of the few commentators in this tool who can be profitably read by scholars of any theological persuasion, because the Greek is the Greek regardless of confessional commitments.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Word Pictures in the New Testament (6 vols., 1930–1933)The primary source for Robertson notes in this tool. Verse-by-verse grammatical commentary through every NT book. Public domain.

A Grammar of the Greek New Testament in the Light of Historical Research (1914)The magnum opus. 1,454 pages covering every aspect of Koine Greek grammar. Still a standard reference work.

Studies in the Text of the New Testament (1926)Essays on specific textual and grammatical questions in the NT, showing Robertson at his most precise.

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body:
Robertson notes apply to New Testament chapters only. Coverage begins with Matthew 1 and will expand as chapters are built out.

sarna.name: Nahum Sarna
sarna.tradition: 1923–2005 ·Jewish Biblical Scholar· Brandeis University · JPS Torah Commentary Series Editor

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Nahum Sarna was born in London in 1923 and received his rabbinic ordination and academic training in England before emigrating to the United States. He earned his doctorate from Dropsie College (later merged into the University of Pennsylvania) and taught for many years at Brandeis University, where he became one of the most respected biblical scholars in North America.

Sarna was the general editor of the Jewish Publication Society Torah Commentary series — the most significant modern Jewish commentary on the Pentateuch in English. His own volumes on Genesis (1989) and Exodus (1991) in that series are considered landmarks of Jewish biblical scholarship: rigorous in philology, deeply informed by rabbinic tradition, and consistently attentive to the ancient Near Eastern world in which the text was composed.

He also wrote “Understanding Genesis” (1966) and “Exploring Exodus” (1986), accessible introductions to the theological and historical dimensions of the first two books of the Torah. These works brought serious scholarship to a broad audience without sacrificing depth.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Sarna reads the Hebrew Bible as a Jewish scholar formed by both the rabbinic tradition and modern historical-critical scholarship. He is exceptionally attentive to the Hebrew text itself — its vocabulary, syntax, wordplay, and the semantic range of key terms. He insists that the text must be understood first in its own linguistic and cultural context before any theological conclusions are drawn.

His distinctive contribution is the consistent situating of biblical texts within their ancient Near Eastern environment. Where a Babylonian creation myth or Egyptian administrative practice illuminates a biblical passage, Sarna brings that material to bear with precision. But he is equally careful to identify where the biblical text diverges from or polemicises against its surrounding culture — where Israel’s theology is not merely parallel to but deliberately counter to the dominant cosmologies of its world.

Sarna also draws deeply on the Jewish interpretive tradition — rabbinic midrash, medieval commentators like Rashi and Nachmanides, and the accumulated insights of Jewish readers over two millennia. This gives his commentary a dimension entirely absent from most Christian scholarship: the voice of the tradition that has read this text as Scripture longer than any other.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Sarna writes from within the Conservative Jewish tradition, which combines commitment to the Torah as sacred Scripture with openness to historical-critical methods. He does not read the text as inerrant in the Protestant sense, but he reads it as authoritative, coherent, and theologically profound. His scholarship is neither purely academic nor narrowly confessional — it occupies the productive middle ground between devotion and rigour that characterises the best Jewish biblical scholarship.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Genesis (JPS Torah Commentary, 1989)His masterwork. Verse-by-verse commentary combining philology, ANE comparative study, and rabbinic tradition. The primary source for Sarna notes in this tool.

Exodus (JPS Torah Commentary, 1991)The companion volume, equally authoritative on the Exodus narrative and the Sinai legislation.

Understanding Genesis (1966)An accessible introduction to the theological and literary dimensions of Genesis, written for the intelligent general reader.

Exploring Exodus (1986)A thematic and theological survey of the Exodus narrative, particularly strong on the historical and cultural background.

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body:
Sarna notes appear throughout Genesis and Exodus. His JPS Torah Commentary covers Genesis and Exodus only — Ruth and Proverbs use different scholars.
