sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Martin J. Selman served as Director of Postgraduate Studies at Spurgeon's College, London, and as a lecturer in Old Testament at the college for many years. His academic focus on the historical books of the Old Testament, particularly Chronicles, made him one of the leading evangelical voices on the Chronicler's theology and purpose.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His two-volume commentary on 1 & 2 Chronicles in the Tyndale Old Testament Commentary (TOTC) series (1994) is widely regarded as one of the best mid-level evangelical treatments of Chronicles. Selman excels at making the Chronicler's theological agenda accessible — explaining why the genealogies matter, why David is presented differently than in Samuel, and how the post-exilic community found identity in these texts.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Selman reads Chronicles as purposeful theological history rather than a defective retelling of Samuel-Kings. He emphasises the Chronicler's distinctive themes: "all Israel" as a united worshipping community, David as liturgist rather than warrior, the centrality of the temple, and the Levites as guardians of Israel's spiritual heritage. His commentary balances careful historical research with pastoral application.

tigay.name: Jeffrey H. Tigay
tigay.tradition: 1941– ·Jewish Biblical Scholar· University of Pennsylvania · JPS Torah Commentary Deuteronomy

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Jeffrey Tigay is the A. M. Ellis Professor Emeritus of Hebrew and Semitic Languages and Literature at the University of Pennsylvania, one of America’s most distinguished universities for biblical and ancient Near Eastern studies.

His 1996 JPS Torah Commentary on Deuteronomy is the definitive modern Jewish scholarly commentary on the book in English. It stands in the tradition established by Nahum Sarna’s Genesis and Exodus volumes: combining rigorous Hebrew philology, deep engagement with the rabbinic interpretive tradition, sensitivity to ancient Near Eastern context, and accessibility to the educated general reader.

Tigay has also written important academic studies on the literary development of the Gilgamesh epic and on the empirical basis of the documentary hypothesis, bringing a comparativist’s perspective to questions of biblical composition.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Tigay’s approach combines the two great streams of Jewish biblical scholarship: the philological-historical tradition of the modern academy and the hermeneutical tradition of the Jewish community. He is alert to the text’s linguistic and historical dimensions while consistently asking what the text has meant to Jewish readers across the centuries.

His handling of the Deuteronomic law sections is particularly strong: he illuminates the legal logic of each statute, traces its relationship to the parallel legislation in Exodus and Leviticus, and draws on ancient Near Eastern legal texts for comparative illumination. His notes on the Shema, the Decalogue, and the firstfruits liturgy are among the finest treatments of these passages in any commentary.

On the authorship and composition of Deuteronomy, Tigay is frank about the critical consensus while remaining primarily interested in what the text means as it stands. He treats the question of Mosaic authorship with appropriate scholarly caution, noting where the evidence cuts in different directions.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Tigay writes from within the Jewish scholarly tradition, combining the rigour of the modern academy with the interpretive heritage of his community. His commentary is neither confessional in the evangelical sense nor dismissively critical — it occupies the productive middle ground of Jewish scholarly engagement with the Torah as both historical document and living religious text.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Deuteronomy (JPS Torah Commentary, 1996)The primary source for Tigay notes in this tool. Comprehensive commentary on the full text with exceptional linguistic and comparative depth.

The Evolution of the Gilgamesh Epic (1982)A landmark comparative study demonstrating how literary compositions develop over time, with implications for understanding biblical compilation.

Empirical Models for Biblical Criticism (ed., 1985)An important collection of essays testing documentary hypothesis claims against known examples of ancient Near Eastern textual development.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
David Toshio Tsumura is Professor Emeritus at Japan Bible Seminary in Tokyo. He earned his PhD from Brandeis University, where he studied under Cyrus Gordon, one of the great Semitists of the twentieth century. This training gave Tsumura an exceptional command of comparative Semitic philology — Akkadian, Ugaritic, and Northwest Semitic languages alongside Hebrew.

His NICOT commentary on 1 Samuel (published as 'The First Book of Samuel,' 2007) is noted for its detailed philological work and its engagement with ancient Near Eastern parallels. He has also published significant research on Genesis 1–2, particularly the relationship between the Hebrew creation account and Mesopotamian creation literature.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Tsumura's distinctive strength is comparative Semitic philology. When a Hebrew word or phrase in 1 Samuel is obscure, he draws on Akkadian, Ugaritic, and other Semitic cognates to illuminate its meaning. This gives his commentary a precision that few others can match on questions of vocabulary and grammar.

He is also attentive to the narrative artistry of 1 Samuel — the characterisation of Saul, David, Samuel, and Jonathan, the use of irony and foreshadowing, and the theological themes that run through the transition from judgeship to monarchy.

Tsumura reads the text as a coherent narrative composition rather than a patchwork of conflicting sources, though he engages seriously with source-critical arguments and explains where he departs from them.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Tsumura writes from an evangelical perspective, treating the biblical text as authoritative Scripture. His Japanese academic context gives his work a distinctive international flavour — he brings a global perspective to English-language Old Testament scholarship. His work is rigorous, precise, and oriented toward the text's meaning rather than toward theological polemic.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The First Book of Samuel (NICOT, 2007) — his major commentary, distinguished by its philological depth and ancient Near Eastern parallels.

Creation and Destruction: A Reappraisal of the Chaoskampf Theory in the Old Testament (1994, revised 2005) — an influential study arguing against reading Genesis 1 through the lens of Babylonian combat mythology.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Willem VanGemeren is Professor Emeritus of Old Testament and Semitic Languages at Trinity Evangelical Divinity School. He edited the five-volumeNew International Dictionary of Old Testament Theology and Exegesis(NIDOTTE), one of the standard reference works for OT studies.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His EBC Psalms (2008) is a comprehensive evangelical commentary that integrates ancient Near Eastern background, Hebrew philology, and Reformed theological reflection.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: VanGemeren reads the Psalms as both historical worship documents and prophetic texts pointing to Christ. He traces royal, messianic, and eschatological themes through the Psalter while attending to each psalm’s literary structure and emotional texture.

waltke.name: Bruce K. Waltke
waltke.tradition: 1930– ·Evangelical OT Scholar· Regent College · Reformed Theological Seminary · NICOT Proverbs

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Bruce K. Waltke is one of the most distinguished evangelical Old Testament scholars of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Born in 1930, he received his doctorate from Harvard University and has taught at Dallas Theological Seminary, Regent College (Vancouver), and Reformed Theological Seminary, among others. His career spans over six decades of productive scholarship at the intersection of Hebrew linguistics, OT theology, and evangelical faith.

His two-volume commentary on Proverbs in the NICOT series (2004–2005) is the most comprehensive evangelical-scholarly treatment of the book in English. It brings to Proverbs the full weight of Waltke’s linguistic expertise — he is co-author of the standard reference grammar of Biblical Hebrew (Waltke-O’Connor) — combined with deep engagement with ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature and rigorous biblical-theological interpretation.

Waltke has also made major contributions to OT theology more broadly, including a systematic OT theology co-authored with Charles Yu, and foundational work on the Psalms, Micah, and the Pentateuch.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Waltke approaches Proverbs as a unified literary and theological work rather than a miscellaneous collection of folk wisdom. He is particularly attentive to the book’s macrostructure — the programmatic role of Proverbs 1–9 (the extended wisdom poems) in establishing the theological framework within which the individual proverbs of chs. 10–31 are to be read.

His philological work on Hebrew vocabulary in Proverbs is unmatched in evangelical scholarship. He treats each term with the same rigour he brings to his Hebrew grammar — attending to semantic range, cognate languages, usage patterns, and contextual nuance. This makes his commentary an indispensable lexical resource as well as a theological one.

Waltke reads Proverbs within a canonical-theological framework: the wisdom of Proverbs is not a secular pragmatism but a Spirit-given perception of God’s created order. The fear of the Lord is the epistemological foundation; all true wisdom flows from right relationship with the covenant God.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body:
Waltke writes from within the Reformed evangelical tradition — committed to the inerrancy and authority of Scripture, to historical-grammatical exegesis, and to the canonical unity of the Old and New Testaments. His theology of wisdom in Proverbs connects the book to the broader biblical-theological narrative: the wisdom personified in Prov 8 is a pointer toward Christ as the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:30; Col 2:3).

He engages critical scholarship rigorously without being governed by its methodological scepticism. His commentaries are always philologically responsible and historically informed while remaining confessionally evangelical.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The Book of Proverbs: Chapters 1–15 (NICOT, 2004)Volume one of the definitive evangelical commentary on Proverbs. Covers the programmatic wisdom poems of chs. 1–9 and the first collection of Solomonic proverbs. The primary source for Waltke notes in this tool.

The Book of Proverbs: Chapters 15–31 (NICOT, 2005)Volume two. Completes the Solomonic collection and the later appendices including the Sayings of Agur and the poem of the ḥēset ḥayil (the woman of valor, Prov 31:10–31).

An Introduction to Biblical Hebrew Syntax (1990)Co-authored with M. P. O’Connor. The standard advanced reference grammar of Biblical Hebrew for English-reading students — “Waltke-O’Connor” is the indispensable technical grammar of the field.

An Old Testament Theology (2007)Co-authored with Charles Yu. A comprehensive biblical-theological treatment of the OT canon, situating individual books within the overarching narrative of creation, covenant, and redemption.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Barry G. Webb is a retired Senior Research Fellow at Moore Theological College in Sydney, Australia, one of the leading evangelical theological institutions in the Anglican world. He earned his PhD from the University of Sheffield with a dissertation on the literary structure and theology of Judges.

Webb's NICOT (New International Commentary on the Old Testament) volume on Judges (2012) is considered one of the finest evangelical commentaries on the book. He also authored an earlier monograph, 'The Book of Judges: An Integrated Reading' (1987), and has contributed to TOTC volumes. His work on the prophets includes 'The Message of Isaiah' in the Bible Speaks Today series.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Webb reads Judges as a carefully crafted literary whole with a deliberate theological argument. His central thesis is that the book traces a pattern of progressive deterioration — not merely cyclical repetition but a downward spiral in Israel's spiritual and moral life from the relatively faithful Othniel to the deeply compromised Samson and the horrifying appendix chapters.

He pays close attention to the book's literary architecture: its framing devices, its internal cross-references, and the way individual judge narratives echo and subvert one another. He is especially insightful on irony and the gap between what characters say and what the narrative reveals about them.

Webb holds the text together as a unified composition rather than dissecting it into hypothetical source documents. This canonical approach allows him to trace theological themes — the nature of true leadership, the danger of syncretism, the faithfulness of God amid human failure — across the full arc of the book.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Webb writes from within the Reformed evangelical tradition shaped by Moore College's distinctively expository culture. He treats the text as Scripture — authoritative, purposeful, and coherent — while engaging seriously with historical and literary scholarship. His commentary is theologically rich without being polemical, and consistently oriented toward the text's message for believing communities.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The Book of Judges (NICOT, 2012) — his major commentary, combining literary analysis with theological exposition.

The Book of Judges: An Integrated Reading (1987) — his doctoral monograph, laying the foundation for his canonical-literary approach.

The Message of Isaiah (Bible Speaks Today, 1996) — an accessible expository commentary on Isaiah.

williamson.name: H.G.M. Williamson
williamson.tradition: Regius Professor of Hebrew, Oxford & Ezra-Nehemiah Authority

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Hugh G.M. Williamson (1947–) is Regius Professor of Hebrew at the University of Oxford, one of the most prestigious Old Testament chairs in the world. A fellow of the British Academy, he has spent his career on the historical books of the Hebrew Bible, with particular expertise in Chronicles, Ezra-Nehemiah, and Isaiah.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: His Word Biblical Commentary on Ezra, Nehemiah (1985) is the standard academic reference for these books, combining rigorous textual analysis with theological sensitivity. He is also author of1 and 2 Chroniclesin the New Century Bible Commentary (1982) andIsrael in the Books of Chronicles(1977). His work on the Aramaic sections of Ezra is particularly authoritative, drawing on Persian administrative documents to illuminate the biblical text.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: Williamson combines critical method with theological seriousness. He is attentive to the Persian-period historical context that most commentators skim past — the administrative structures, the Aramaic formulae, the imperial policies that shaped Jewish restoration. His textual work is meticulous: he distinguishes editorial layers without losing sight of the final form's theological message. He reads Ezra-Nehemiah as purposeful literature, not merely as a historical source to be mined for facts.

wiseman.name: A.A. Anderson
wiseman.tradition: 1927–2009 ·OT Scholar· University of Manchester · WBC 2 Samuel

sections.title: Biography
sections.body: Donald John Wiseman (1918–2010) was Professor of Assyriology at the University of London and one of the foremost Assyriologists of the twentieth century. A committed evangelical Christian, he uniquely combined deep expertise in cuneiform texts and ancient Near Eastern archaeology with careful biblical scholarship. He served as chairman of the British School of Archaeology in Iraq and edited the journalIraqfor decades.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body: Wiseman reads 1–2 Kings with unmatched command of the Assyrian, Babylonian, and Egyptian source material that illuminates the biblical narrative. His TOTC commentary integrates archaeological discoveries — seal impressions, annals, king lists, treaty forms — to show how the biblical text fits within the broader ancient Near Eastern world. He treats the text as historically reliable while acknowledging interpretive difficulties.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Wiseman writes from a conservative evangelical perspective, affirming the historical reliability of the biblical narrative while engaging seriously with ancient Near Eastern evidence. His approach is characterized by meticulous philological care and a conviction that archaeology consistently supports rather than undermines the biblical record.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body: 1 and 2 Kings(Tyndale OT Commentary, 1993) — Concise, archaeologically informed commentary. Also:Chronicles of Chaldaean Kings(1956),Nebuchadrezzar and Babylon(1985).

sections.title: Appears In
sections.body: Wiseman notes appear throughout 1 Kings and 2 Kings.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Walter Brueggemann (born 1933) is one of the most influential Old Testament scholars of the past half-century. He spent the bulk of his career at Columbia Theological Seminary in Decatur, Georgia, where he held the William Marcellus McPheeters Chair of Old Testament. Before Columbia he taught at Eden Theological Seminary in Missouri.

Brueggemann is extraordinarily prolific, with more than one hundred books to his name. His 1978 work 'The Prophetic Imagination' became foundational reading across mainline Protestant seminaries, and his 'Theology of the Old Testament' (1997) proposed a rhetorical-dialogical approach that reads the Hebrew Bible as a sustained argument between competing testimonies about God. His commentaries on Jeremiah — in the International Theological Commentary and the 'To Build, to Plant' Eerdmans series — bring his theological vision to bear on the prophet whose voice most closely matches his own.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Brueggemann reads the prophets — and Jeremiah above all — as voices of counter-testimony against the dominant ideology of their cultures. His framework distinguishes between 'royal consciousness' (the established power's theology of permanence and divine favour) and 'prophetic consciousness' (the disruptive insistence that God is free, grief-stricken, and active in dismantling unjust arrangements).

His Jeremiah commentary reads the prophet as an embodiment of this prophetic consciousness: one who simultaneously grieves, accuses, and dares to imagine a future beyond destruction. Brueggemann is attentive to rhetoric and literary form, but his primary interest is theological — what does this text claim about the character of God and the shape of faithful life in an empire?

He consistently foregrounds the political dimension of prophetic speech, reading Jeremiah's confrontations with kings and priests as models for communities that must speak truth to power. His work is unapologetically pastoral — he writes for preachers, teachers, and communities seeking to live faithfully.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Brueggemann stands in the Reformed tradition within mainline Protestantism, shaped by Karl Barth's emphasis on the freedom and sovereignty of God. He is theologically progressive, open to historical-critical methods, and consistently attentive to the social-justice dimensions of the biblical text. His scholarship blends rigorous academic analysis with pastoral and homiletical concern — he writes as a scholar who preaches and a preacher who does serious scholarship.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
The Prophetic Imagination (1978) — his most famous work, providing the theoretical framework that undergirds all his prophetic commentary.

A Commentary on Jeremiah: Exile and Homecoming (1998) — International Theological Commentary series, combining literary sensitivity with theological depth.

To Build, to Plant: A Commentary on Jeremiah 26–52 (1991) — Eerdmans series, covering the latter half of Jeremiah with particular attention to the hope oracles.

Theology of the Old Testament: Testimony, Dispute, Advocacy (1997) — his magnum opus, proposing a dialectical reading of Israel's core testimony and counter-testimony about God.

sections.title: Biography
sections.body:
Jack R. Lundbom is an American biblical scholar who has devoted the greater part of his career to the book of Jeremiah. He completed his doctoral work at Union Theological Seminary in New York under James Muilenburg, the scholar who pioneered rhetorical criticism as a method of biblical study. Muilenburg's influence is stamped on everything Lundbom writes — the conviction that the artistry of biblical prose and poetry is not ornament but meaning.

Lundbom's three-volume Anchor Bible Commentary on Jeremiah (1999, 2004, 2004) is the most comprehensive English-language commentary on the book. It covers Jeremiah 1–20, 21–36, and 37–52 in exhaustive philological and rhetorical detail. The Anchor Bible series is one of the premier academic commentary series in English, and Lundbom's contribution stands as its definitive treatment of Jeremiah.

sections.title: Interpretive Approach
sections.body:
Lundbom's primary method is rhetorical criticism — the close analysis of how the text uses repetition, inclusio, chiasm, keyword chains, and other structural devices to communicate meaning. He is especially attentive to the way Jeremiah's poetry works: its parallelism, its sound patterns, its deliberate breaks and resumptions.

He combines this literary sensitivity with rigorous philological work. His commentary provides detailed analysis of Hebrew grammar, vocabulary, and syntax at every turn. Where textual variants exist between the Masoretic Text and the Septuagint (which is substantially shorter for Jeremiah), Lundbom works through the evidence carefully.

While historically informed, Lundbom's approach is primarily text-centred. He is less interested in reconstructing the 'historical Jeremiah' than in understanding how the text in its received form communicates its message. This makes his commentary both scholarly and practically useful for close readers of the biblical text.

sections.title: Theological Tradition
sections.body: Lundbom works within the critical-academic tradition, writing for scholars and serious students rather than for a confessional audience. His commentary does not advance a particular theological agenda; it seeks to let the text speak on its own terms through careful literary and philological analysis. He treats the biblical text with respect and seriousness without imposing dogmatic frameworks upon it.

sections.title: Key Works
sections.body:
Jeremiah 1–20 (Anchor Bible, 1999) — The first volume, establishing his rhetorical-critical method and covering the early oracles and the confessions of Jeremiah.

Jeremiah 21–36 (Anchor Bible, 2004) — Covers the prose sermons, the conflict narratives, and the Book of Consolation (chs 30–33).

Jeremiah 37–52 and Baruch (Anchor Bible, 2004) — The fall of Jerusalem narratives, the Egyptian flight, and the oracles against the nations.

Jeremiah: A Study in Ancient Hebrew Rhetoric (1975, revised 1997) — His foundational rhetorical study that laid the groundwork for the commentary.
